text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic left colectomy, executing a complete mesocolic excision while preserving the inferior mesenteric vein. An intracorporeal anastomosis is being prepared.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic left colectomy, executing a complete mesocolic excision while preserving the inferior mesenteric vein. Intracorporeal anastomosis is carried out within the surgical field.
A six to eight years old male with adenocarcinoma of the descending colon. Colonoscopy shows a six centimeter villous lesion and CT shows a five centimeters while thickening in the descending colon.
A six to eight years old male with adenocarcinoma of the descending colon. Colonoscopy shows a six centimeter villous lesion and CT shows a five centimeters while thickening in the descending colon.
An intraoperative colonoscopy is performed to visually confirm the position of the lesion in the descending colon, and the lesion is marked for reference.
An intraoperative colonoscopy is performed to visually confirm the position of the lesion in the descending colon, followed by marking the lesion's location.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection between the artery and vein, detaching the descending mesocolon from the retroperitoneum. The origin of the inferior mesenteric artery is identified, and surrounding fat and lymph nodes are removed, including those around the left colic branch. Dissection continues until the inferior mesenteric vein is visualized crossing the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection between the artery and vein, detaching the descending mesocolon from the retroperitoneum. The origin of the inferior mesenteric artery is identified, and surrounding fat and lymph nodes are removed, including those around the left colic branch. The dissection continues until the inferior mesenteric vein is seen crossing the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon uses the inferior mesenteric vein as a landmark to begin dissection, detaching it from the descending mesocolon. Fat surrounding the vein is removed, and all branches entering the descending mesocolon are ligated, with the vein preserved in place.

The surgeon uses the vein as a landmark to begin dissection, detaching it from the descending mesocolon. Fat surrounding the vein is removed, and all branches entering the descending mesocolon are ligated, with the intention of preserving the vein in place.
The surgeon preserves the inferior mesenteric vein in place to facilitate venous drainage of the distal descending colon and sigmoid, preparing for a higher anastomosis.

The surgeon preserves the inferior mesenteric vein in place, facilitating venous drainage of the distal descending colon and sigmoid, which allows for a higher anastomosis during the laparoscopic left colectomy.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection and creates a window to access the lesser sac. The preserved vein is visible below the pancreas as the surgeon prepares to dissect the last branch entering the mesocolon.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection and proceeds to create a window to access the lesser sac. The preserved vein is visible below the pancreas as the surgeon prepares to dissect the last branch entering the mesocolon.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection above the pancreas, detaching the descending and transverse mesocolon from the pancreas. The coloepiploic ligaments are detached from the distal transverse mesocolon, and the splenic flexure is separated from above. Lateral mobilization is completed to fully detach the left colon.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection above the pancreas, detaching the descending and transverse mesocolon from the pancreatic surface. The coloepiploic ligaments are detached from the distal transverse mesocolon, and the splenic flexure is separated from above. Lateral mobilization is completed to fully detach the left colon.

The surgeon selects the point for distal transection of the colon, ensuring it is at least ten centimeters from the tumor. Similarly, a point is chosen on the transverse mesocolon, ten centimeters proximal to the tumor. The preserved vein facilitates a higher anastomosis.

The surgeon selects the point for distal transection of the colon, ensuring it is at least ten centimeters from the tumor. Similarly, a point is chosen on the transverse mesocolon, ten centimeters proximal to the tumor.

The surgeon detaches the marginal arcade on both sides and uses a stapler to transect the colon, leaving the specimen in place. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed, with the vein preserved to facilitate higher anastomosis.
The surgeon detaches the marginal arcade on both sides and uses a stapler to transect the colon, leaving the specimen in place. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed.
The surgeon closes the surgical opening using a first layer of barbed suture, followed by a second layer of running suture to reinforce the closure.
The surgeon closes the hole using a first line of barbed suture, followed by a second layer of running suture to reinforce the closure.
The surgeon inspects the integrity of the vein and its drainage, ensuring proper function. The surgical specimen is displayed for examination.
